original_abstract: Abstract Computing the food component (nutrient) amount in 100 kilocalories, 100 grams or 100 milliliters, the reference amount customarily consumed (RACC), or 50 grams of food demonstrates the food component amount of some foods unsuitably. So, selecting some foods based on them may elevate the hazards of some chronic diseases. Computing the food component amount and assessing suitable levels of food components and the nutritional quality according to the Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC), the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA), and the suggested procedure were implemented on 8,596 food cases, 29 food components, and 25 food categories. Selecting some foods under the FDA and CAC to reach sufficient intakes of positive food components surpassed energy demands. Selecting some foods under the CAC did not satisfy the demands of positive food components. Some foods that satisfied the demands of positive food components were not suitable food selections under the CAC. Selecting some foods under the FDA or CAC surpassed the demands of negative food components (including cholesterol, energy, fat, saturated fat, and sodium). Some foods that did not surpass the demands of negative food components were not suitable food selections under the CAC or FDA. Due to the vulnerabilities of selecting foods on the basis of the reference amounts of food, fast foods under the CAC and FDA in serving size (the serving size or serving is obtained from the RACC), spices and herbs under the CAC in 100 grams or 100 milliliters, and vegetables and vegetable products under the CAC in 100 kilocalories obtained the highest average scores for nutritional quality based on positive food components (including vitamins, protein, dietary fiber, and minerals, excluding sodium) among food categories for children aged four years and older and adults. Graphical Abstract
humanized_abstract: Based on the amount of food components in 100 kcal, 100g or 100ml of food as the Reference Amount Customarily Consumed (RACC) or 50g of food, the amount of certain food components in some foods cannot be obtained reasonably. Then, the foods that can be reasonably selected for reducing the risk of chronic diseases are discussed. By implementing the method of calculating the food components, determining the suitable levels of food components and evaluating the nutritional quality according to the Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) and the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) guidelines, 8,596 food samples in 25 food categories and 29 food components were evaluated. According to the FDA or CAC guidelines, the food components selected from some reasonable foods met the requirements of positive nutrients for sufficient intake, while the CAC guideline did not meet the requirements of positive nutrients. Conversely, the food components selected from some reasonable foods that met the requirements of positive nutrients did not meet the criteria of CAC guideline for negative nutrients such as cholesterol, energy, fat, saturated fat and sodium. Moreover, the food components selected from some unreasonable foods did not meet the criteria of CAC guideline for negative nutrients. Finally, the basis for selecting foods based on RACC has been proved to be inferior for being fast foods based on FDA/CAC serving size (obtained from RACC), as well as spices and herbs based on the CAC in 100g or 100ml and as for vegetables and vegetable products based on the CAC in 100kcal. The former three types of food categories are found to have the highest average scores of positive nutrients for nutritional quality of food among children ≥ 4 years and adults.
